title: Pay Yourself First
summary: The single habit that makes saving actually happen.
doThisToday: Pick a percentage (even just 5%) and move that much from any money you have right now into a separate savings spot.

cards.prompt: Most people save whatever's left at the end of the month. Usually, that's nothing.
cards.explanation: "Pay yourself first" flips the order: the moment money comes in, a chunk goes straight to savings — before a single dollar gets spent on anything else.

cards.prompt: You get a $200 paycheck. Which approach is "pay yourself first"?
options: Spend on whatever you want all month, save whatever happens to be left over
Immediately move $20 (10%) to savings the day you're paid, then spend from the remaining $180
Wait until the end of the month to decide if you have extra to save
cards.explanation: Savings gets treated like a required bill you pay to yourself — not an optional leftover. That one order-of-operations change is why it works so much better.

cards.prompt: True or false: "pay yourself first" means saving whatever's left after you've spent on everything else.
cards.explanation: That's the opposite — that's how most people accidentally save nothing. Pay yourself first means savings comes out FIRST, before spending money on wants.

cards.prompt: You get a $200 paycheck and decide to pay yourself first at 10%. How many dollars go straight to savings before you spend anything?
cards.explanation: 10% of $200 is $20 — moved to savings immediately, no debate about it later in the month when temptation is higher.

cards.prompt: The upgrade: automate it so you don't have to rely on willpower every single time.
cards.explanation: Most banking apps let you set up an automatic transfer the day you get paid — savings happens without you having to remember or resist temptation.

cards.prompt: Why does automating your savings transfer usually work better than "I'll just remember to save some"?
options: It removes the decision entirely, so willpower and memory aren't required
It doesn't actually work better, it's the same thing
Automation saves more money than manual transfers, mathematically
cards.explanation: Willpower runs out, especially by the end of a long day of temptations. A system that doesn't need willpower is a system that actually works long-term.

cards.prompt: Put a "pay yourself first" paycheck in the right order.
cards.explanation: The whole system only works because the transfer happens BEFORE spending starts — reverse the order and you're back to "save whatever's left," which is usually nothing.

cards.prompt: There's a real psychological reason willpower-based plans fail by evening.
cards.frontText: What do psychologists call the idea that willpower gets used up like a limited resource throughout the day?
cards.backText: Ego depletion. The theory is that resisting temptation repeatedly — a big lunch, a tempting purchase, a hard conversation — leaves less willpower for the next decision. It's debated among researchers, but it matches most people's real experience: it's way easier to make a good money decision at 9am than at 9pm.
cards.explanation: This is exactly why automation beats willpower — a system that runs automatically doesn't care what time of day it is or how tired you are.

cards.prompt: Start with any percentage you can actually stick to — even 5%.
cards.explanation: A small amount you save every single time beats a big amount you plan to save but never actually do.
